figures.caption: Table 1: Overall evaluation results for prototypes. For semantic geometry retention evaluation, score 1-2 indicates little or no preservation of corresponding geometry. A score of 3 indicates that the target shape and proportions are correctly recovered, but fine-grained interfaces (e.g., blade edges, pin slots) are smoothed or imprecise. Scores 4–5 indicate that functional and risk-relevant cues are clearly preserved with minimal geometric loss. For harmful-cue preservation, the left value reports the mean across VLMs; the right reports the per-sample maximum.
Figure 9: Adversarial robustness of input filters.
Figure 10: Comparison of attention maps under clean, adversarial, and camouflaging conditions.
Figure 16: Examples of degraded images rejected by the VAST API due to content policy violations. Left: Knuckleduster with level-3 noise. Right: Key with level-1 (top) and level-2 (bottom) blur.
Figure 17: Quantitative evaluation of generated geometric with semantically camouflaged inputs.
Figure 4: Box plot for harmful-cue preservation of the generated geometries in each category.
Figure 3: The qualitative comparisons for image-to-3D generation. The left column shows the input images.
Table 6: Measuring results of the printed objects.

Figure 8: Box plot of quality and harmful-cue preservation of the generated objects via original and semantically camouflaged inputs. The statistical significance is assessed using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test (∗: p < 0.05, ∗∗: p < 0.01).

Figure 7: Functionality evaluation of the printed 3D objects. For each object, the left and center figures show the quantitative measurements, while the right figure demonstrates risk-relevant physical effects.

Figure 5: Box plot of harmful-cue preservation (red) and reference alignment (blue) of the generated objects from degraded inputs across three severity levels. The colored values below each level indicate the respective means. Statistical significance between adjacent levels is assessed using the Wilcoxon signedrank test (∗: p < 0.05, ∗∗: p < 0.01, ∗∗∗: p < 0.001).
Figure 6: Evaluation of quality and harmful-cue preservation of the generated geometries via inputs of different views.
Figure 2: Upper: Prototype and augmentation. Lower:VLMbased and human evaluation demonstration.

Table 3: Inclusion and exclusion criteria for benchmark construction. The benchmark is designed as a mechanism-oriented stress test for geometry-mediated misuse, rather than an exhaustive inventory of harmful physical objects or an estimate of real-world misuse prevalence.

Table 5: Pairwise comparison of harmful-cue preservation using the Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test. Mean Diff indicates the average score difference (Model A - Model B). A positive difference implies Model A is more hazardous. P-values in bold (< 0.05) indicate statistically significant differences.
Figure 15: Quantitative evaluation of generated geometric under degraded quality.

Figure 14: VLM Sanity Check. As critical geometric constraints are progressively corrupted (from Original to Severe), the VLM’s Hazard Levels monotonically decrease, demonstrating its sensitivity to functional details over mere global shapes.
